title: Specificity Beats Cleverness
concept: Why vague prompts produce vague answers; concrete constraints, formats, and examples reduce ambiguity.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: In the last lesson, which building block of a prompt tells the AI what shape or style the final answer should take?
exercises.explain: Output describes the desired form of the response — like 'a bulleted list' or 'a 2-paragraph summary.' Instructions tell the AI what to do, and Input is the material it works on, but Output shapes what comes back to you.

cards.title: Why Did I Get THAT Answer?
cards.body: Ever asked an AI something simple and gotten a weirdly random or off-topic reply? It's usually not the AI's fault — it's because the question left too much open to interpretation.

cards.title: What Specificity Means
cards.body: A specific prompt names exact details: topic, audience, length, tone, and format. Vague prompts leave these open, so the AI has to guess — and guesses vary wildly from one run to the next.

cards.title: Ordering Pizza
cards.body: Saying 'make me food' could get you anything from a salad to a hot dog. Saying 'a medium pepperoni pizza, thin crust, cut into 8 slices' gets you exactly what you wanted. Prompts work the same way — the more specific your order, the less guessing the kitchen (the AI) has to do.

cards.title: Same Question, Two Ways
cards.body: Vague: 'Write about dogs.' You might get a poem, a history essay, or a list of breeds. Specific: 'Write a 3-sentence description of why beagles make good family pets, for a pet adoption website.' Now there's only one reasonable answer to give.

cards.title: Myth: Clever Wording Beats Clear Wording
cards.body: Some people think fancy or witty phrasing gets better AI results. Actually, plain, precise instructions beat clever language almost every time — clarity reduces guessing far more than creativity does.

cards.title: Vague vs. Specific Prompt
cards.body: Compare how much guessing each prompt leaves the AI to do.

left.text: 'Write a story.' — Topic? Length? Audience? Tone? All unknown, so results vary wildly each time.

right.text: 'Write a 150-word bedtime story about a brave turtle, for a 6-year-old, with a happy ending.' — Everything needed is defined.

exercises.prompt: Which change would make this prompt MORE specific: 'Summarize this article'?

options.text: Specifying 'in 3 bullet points, focused on the financial impact, for a busy executive'

exercises.explain: Adding concrete constraints — format (bullet points), focus (financial impact), and audience (busy executive) — removes ambiguity about what kind of summary is wanted. Politeness, brevity, or fancy words don't reduce guessing the way real constraints do.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Using longer, more sophisticated-sounding words in a prompt generally makes AI responses more accurate.
exercises.explain: False. Accuracy comes from clear constraints — topic, format, length, audience — not fancy vocabulary. A short, plain, specific prompt usually outperforms a long, flowery, vague one.

exercises.sentence: Vague prompts produce vague answers because the AI has to fill gaps with guesses instead of following clear constraints.
exercises.explain: Constraints (like length, format, and audience) tell the AI exactly what boundaries to work within. Without them, the model fills the gap with its own guess, which may not match what you wanted.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three prompts was written by someone who understands 'specificity beats cleverness'?

options.text: 'Compose an eloquent and masterful discourse on the nature of productivity, dazzling the reader with wit.'
'List 5 productivity tips for remote workers, each under 15 words, formatted as a numbered list.'
'Tell me something useful about being productive, make it good.'

exercises.explain: Option B specifies the number of tips, the audience, the length limit, and the format — leaving little room for misinterpretation. The other two focus on sounding impressive or vague quality ('good,' 'eloquent') instead of giving the AI clear boundaries to work within.

exercises.prompt: You're building a homework-helper bot. Which prompt is MOST likely to produce a consistently useful, on-target answer every time?

options.text: 'Explain how to find the area of a rectangle to a 5th grader, using one real-world example, in under 80 words.'
'Be a really smart and clever math teacher.'

exercises.explain: Option B wins because it nails down the topic (area of a rectangle), the audience (5th grader), the format (one real-world example), and the length (under 80 words). The other options sound fine but leave huge gaps — 'math,' 'shapes,' and 'clever' can mean a thousand different things, so the AI has to guess, and guesses are where quality falls apart. Specificity isn't boring — it's how you turn a lottery ticket into a guarantee.
